speaker: interviewer
text: In the period of 1938 to '39, until '43, how had things changed in your school? Just in life in general, the treatment of the Jews in Munkacs by the Hungarians? What was that all like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was cruel. It's cruel, the same thing. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's like-- it's like you have to talk Hungarian. They didn't like it if you talk another language. You know? Like-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] but generally, everybody in Hungary came in-- Jews were-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] it was bad for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was bad? What was the treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: For everything-- with business, with [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] cops, were terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give you a smack for nothing. You know, they go around and they were like-- you didn't have freedom, like you walk in the street, and you were afraid always. Like in the last, when it started, I think end of '43, there was already you couldn't go out. You had to make it at 6 o'clock. It was all kind of terrible things. You weren't free, like the Czechoslovakians-- that's for sure not. The democratic life in the [INAUDIBLE], it's-- they hated the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of blatant acts of anti-semitism did you experience or witness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were calling all kind of names to Jews-- stinky Jews, you hear that at school, too. We had neighbors, also Hungarian. You know, they came to live-- the Czechoslovakian had to go out, and the houses remained. So they took the houses from the Czechoslovakian people. And we had neighbors there. And there were terrible-- they would say, you know, they had little braids, like something like-- made like that. No, they made fun. And they also threw some stones, also. And my father, he used to go-- he used to like to go early in the morning, you know, before he opens his business, he liked to go. He went with a hack under his arm-- put it in his jacket, and inside. And that's how he went-- because he was afraid. And so early in the morning, sometimes it wasn't even light-- and it was independent. So that's how-- you know, that was how it started. We just weren't free. We couldn't go out when we wanted at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there restrictions? Or was it just dangerous?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was both. It was both-- restrictions and dangerous. After 6 o'clock-- we couldn't-- we didn't go out. We didn't move out from the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of restrictions were imposed upon the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could tell you that at night, we had to be dark, you know-- dark in the house. Not in the house, but to take down the-- they would pull down the shades. And also, we were afraid. It was like starting with the Germans. It was bad. You know, so all around, buying clothes, everything, it stopped. It wasn't the same-- it was-- it was like we started to be very poor. People started to be very poor. And we had so much, so much. And we had-- after that, even the bread was already-- it was-- flour was different. They didn't have the white flour. Everything went a mess. And most of the Hungarians, they took, they had-- but the Jews, it didn't come to us because it wasn't enough. You know, till we came, there wasn't already, there was none. You had to buy the dark flour to have the bread, whatever. It was bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back to your father. What did he tell you when he had finally came back after his year away from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: What he told us?

speaker: interviewer
text: What his experience was?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tortured him there. Life was terrible for him altogether. He missed the family. He knew that the business was down, and he was worried that we should have food enough. And that we could buy clothing, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of torture?

speaker: interviewee
text: They hit him. They made all kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I don't know how to call it in English. You know, you have to get down on the floor, and then go back and-- like that kind of a thing in the morning, until about 12 o'clock. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] there was no food. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he came home, he looked terrible. He came home, he looked like somebody coming home already from the Holocaust. It was terrible. For nothing that he did. I'm sure that boy didn't do nothing, but how it happened that the cops find out and they came to us. I think that the Hungarian neighbors, where they had, they must have say something. They must have see him. And they knew that he even doesn't belong to us. And that could've happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the period--

speaker: interviewee
text: We suffered already before we went to the Holocaust. I'm telling you, we suffered a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the period after your father returned.

speaker: interviewee
text: That my father returned from the place there? [SIGHS] Well, he tried to sell a lot of things what we had, because-- and that was-- that was already-- you know, he was a year locked up. He was-- In 1944, they took us away already. March, was it March? March. They took us away, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came home. He didn't have too much time already. Tell me about when they took you away. Can you describe the events that happened at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had-- that was Passover. We still had our meals, and everything. And the last day, Passover, they came in-- the Hungarian-- I don't how do you describe that they-- it's cops, also, but they had also a different name. They had-- they had some feathers. I don't know. In Jewish, I know, but I don't know in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it in Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jewish? [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. They came in, and just-- we didn't have too much time. And they came and they had guns-- I mean, not guns, the rifle. And they just took it away. And that's it. We had time, a little, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of the clothes, better clothes, to put on. And the rest remained there. And they took us to Kalush. There was bricks-- we made brick there. It was a factory for brick.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was Kalush?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty far. It was out of the city, about an hour maybe. An hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just walked there. No travelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else was with you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was in the group going to Kalush?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was other people too. But us, they took before, because my father was locked up in that thing. So we had also, it [NON-ENGLISH]. That was really in our city, not far from our-- in the city actually, it was also. But, we--they took us there, because my father had a name already that he did something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us there. It was awful. Describe what it was like when you arrived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very dirty there. It was full of sand. Full of-- and we slept on the floor. And they opened there some kind of a kitchen, there, but awful food, awful food. You have to stand and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wait for that little bit of food when they tell you. And there was a lot of people. I don't know exactly how many people were there, but in a lot of places, they were brought in those people. My father was there, locked up, too. He was in a special place there. They locked up-- there was very rich people there. And they locked up those people. And they put my father, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did they provide for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was awful food, really not edible. Soup, some potatoes, maybe. Not enough. And you could also, from Christian people, they used to come in, you know, and they sold you some bread. And we had a little bit money-- everybody took a little bit money with them, and they didn't know what's going to happen, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they ask three times, four times for a bread. And I don't remember exactly. You had to pay a lot of money. But if you had the money, you could buy, because they brought in from the city, the non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, and yeah, and no privacy, absolutely nothing. They had like-- you didn't have toilets. You had a latrina-- I don't know how it's called. You know, everybody went on that. They made a big hole like-- and they put a piece of wood there. And that was it. That was-- you had to go, and things like that. But awful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about for sleeping arrangements? [SHAKES HEAD]

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no sleeping arrangements. Everybody find a place to sleep on the floor. That's it. Full of sand, and dirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any blankets or anything of the sort? [SHAKES HEAD]

speaker: interviewee
text: Slept in your clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Kalush for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four weeks. It was an awful place there, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding Kalush?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an enclosed kind of area? Or was it just guards? How was it set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like, you know, guards watching us. And also the Germans came in. And then we went to Auschwitz, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] that was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] June. June-- was in June. Yeah, I can't remember exactly today. But it's impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe for me the course of events that took place with the deportation from Kalush to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that particular year, we had to wear that-- what do you call it? And that was before, too, when you went out, you had to wear that-- a star.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were in Hungary. In the last-- that last-- before the German came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me what the star looked like? What color it was? What it said on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was white. I think it was written, Jew. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so one day they came and they took us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how they came in-- the course of events that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just came in, and they said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we're going in another place, put us together in a row. And we just-- we just went. And they had the cattles-- the thing they had for us prepared already to go to Auschwitz. And the whole family went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to take your belongings with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You didn't-- you didn't have-- no, you have to leave it, your belongings. But, I mean, your clothes, your things, you had with you. Till we came to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the cattle cars. How many people were in it, what the conditions were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I can't describe it. It was awful. It was unbelievable. One another-- we just didn't have place to sit, not just place to stand even. People were fainting. There was no air. The cattle had-- [MAKES SQUARE SHAPE WITH HANDS]. And people died there, already. My mother, when we came down from-- we were in Auschwitz already. She didn't feel good. Had to hold her hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the journey? How many days?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many days we went there? Three days. There was no water-- nothing, no food. I think we took a little bit food with us, and we had it for-- my older sister, she didn't eat nothing. My mother couldn't eat nothing. We had-- what we had was a piece of bread, but that's it. They didn't give you anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the toilet facility?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no toilet facilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: People are making there, and that's it. What could you do? If somebody had to. It was indescribable stink. You can-- there is no words for that. There was-- I don't know what to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any conversations going on about where you were going, or just conversations around you? What people may have been saying? Or conversations amongst your own family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we said we don't know what-- where we're going, what's going to happen to us. That was the conversation. It's indescribable thing-- that something-- [MUMBLES]. It's not even for animals. This is enough describe. Not even a human-- a live animal, what they did. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me what you saw when you first arrived in Auschwitz? And did you know what Auschwitz was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we arrived, we actually-- in the minute, I didn't know what's going on. My older sister was holding my mother, because she didn't feel good. And my younger was that side because she was afraid, also, my mother doesn't feel good. I was on this side. My sister, who died, was on this side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen-- Helen. She died two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Helen. She was my [INAUDIBLE]. And in a minute, they came, the SS, he came. He had a stick-- he came and made like that. There was my mother, there was my older sister, and there was my younger sister. And I was somehow on this side with my sister, mind you-- I didn't know she was there. I found out later. And I says, in a minute, I says, I belong to that family. And that was it. I didn't see them anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, it was later-- my father, as he was taking him out, and down-- I mean, he came down, he took him to the man. He wasn't there. But after that, as we were-- they put us to march, you know, to the place-- because that was a big place, Auschwitz. I said, that was Birkenau, not Auschwitz. Auschwitz was the city. That was Birkenau. And as we marching, and suddenly we see a big bonfire and there were people-- there were people there, older people, like lying down there. And suddenly, we see that they take those old people and they throw them in the bonfire. So everybody, we were most girls, you know-- everybody started to scream. Horrible, we saw that. And I had on an extra clothes somehow, I don't know, still, you know, from-- brought it from home. And I remain in that. And I figured, well, I should take down my clothes at least, you know, because I had sweaters and whatever I had. Take down my clothes, I shouldn't burn in that fire. The clothes-- it should be easier.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister, Helen, she was screaming terrible. I said to her, look what can we do? We can do nothing. And the scream was absolutely-- I can't describe it. Is unbearable, unbearable. Because they were screaming, put them in the fire. We were screaming. The whole thing was like a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to describe that. And one SS came up, he said like that, listen, if you're going to scream like that, you see that big bonfire? You're going to go there, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he brought out the girl. She was shaved. She had a long dress on, with a German thing on it-- an apron, [NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE]. And he said, if you're going to be quiet, you're going to look like this girl. Anyway, we settled for that, because, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So t was true-- they took us. And that place was a huge, huge place there. And we're shaving, shaving hair, shaving everywhere. We had to pick up our clothes, get something, you know-- everything. There was nothing that you could keep. Who did the shaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Women or men?

speaker: interviewee
text: German girls, German men. There was no difference, you know. It was a horrible thing for us girls, you know. And the dogs, the German dogs, they were all around. All the time, you know, between us. And we settled for that. Then they took us in the blocks. bon oh, yeah, we got a pair of wooden shoes. And of course, no hair, nothing. I didn't recognize my sister after Auschwitz-- I didn't know where. I didn't see her, because, I didn't know, you look so different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, I see her. I said, Helen? You Helen? Well, she says yes, I'm Helen. Let's stop now and go to the next tape.
